articles.title: Thiago da Costa of Procore: 5 Questions
articles.narrative: This article is a Q&A interview with Thiago da Costa, Senior Vice President of AI and Data at Procore, discussing the integration of Datagrid Intelligence's agentic artificial intelligence into Procore's platform. The expanded Procore AI platform aims to automate high-friction construction workflows such as submittals, RFIs, and contract reviews, by enabling the AI to execute tasks directly within the platform. Da Costa emphasizes that this new agentic AI goes beyond previous search capabilities by actively performing work, leveraging multimodal intelligence to process visual data from job sites, and ultimately helping to upskill the construction workforce by providing immediate access to project knowledge.

people.name: Thiago da Costa
people.title: Senior Vice President of AI and Data

articles.title: National Homebuilder M/I Homes Hires Prophetic for Land Evaluation
In the Miami-Area Condo Market, There Are Two Narratives
articles.narrative: The South Florida condo market is experiencing a bifurcated trend: a robust luxury segment driven by an influx of high-net-worth individuals and corporate relocations, contrasted with a struggling lower end facing pressure from new structural repair mandates. Developers of high-end projects, such as the Four Seasons-branded condo in Coconut Grove and the Nobu-branded tower in Brickell, are seeing strong pre-sales and securing significant construction financing, indicating sustained demand for premium products. However, the market for older, more affordable condos is softening due to costly special assessments mandated by post-Surfside reforms, leading to increased inventory and longer selling periods. This dynamic highlights the importance of product differentiation and amenity-rich offerings even within the luxury sector, as buyers are discerning despite high demand.
articles.market_intelligence: Florida's 2022 legislative reforms mandate structural health reports and reserve funding for condo buildings at least three stories tall and 30+ years old, impacting the older condo stock. Previously, only Miami-Dade and Broward counties required recertification, and boards often delayed or waived renovations. The median sale price of a South Florida condo has fallen by 1.5 percent in the past year to $310,000. The millionaire population of Miami and West Palm Beach surged by 94% and 112% respectively between 2014 and 2024. Transactions in South Florida above $10 million and $30 million both doubled year-over-year in Q1 2026.

articles.title: Nashville’s Indy, Corporate Club Owners Both Try to Make Enough at the Door
articles.narrative: Nashville's historic independent music venue scene, particularly along Lower Broadway, is undergoing a significant transformation as rising commercial real estate values and property taxes push out smaller, family-owned establishments in favor of larger, corporate-backed entertainment complexes. While some iconic independent venues persist in areas like SoBro and Green Hills, many face closure risks, prompting community support efforts. This shift reflects the evolving nature of country music towards a more pop-centric sound and the increasing commercialization of the city's entertainment core. Despite the growth of large-scale venues, industry leaders believe there will always be a place for independent artists in Nashville's diverse music ecosystem.

articles.title: Stockdale’s Andrew Saba On Medical Real Estate’s Supply-Demand Imbalance
articles.narrative: Stockdale Capital Partners, a Los Angeles-based firm, is actively expanding its healthcare real estate portfolio through two distinct funds: an open-ended healthcare fund targeting stabilized assets and an opportunistic fund focused on buy-to-suit or office-to-medical conversions. The firm's strategy is driven by the inelastic demand for healthcare services, an aging U.S. population, and a significant supply-demand imbalance in medical outpatient facilities. Rising construction costs and labor expenses have curtailed new supply, leading to increased competition for existing properties, strong occupancy rates in the mid-90s, high tenant retention, and robust rent growth in the sector. Stockdale is primarily focused on the top 50 metro areas, with a preference for Sun Belt and West/Southwest U.S. markets experiencing patient migration.
articles.market_intelligence: The U.S. population is aging, with the peak expected over the next four to six years, driving continued demand for outpatient medical space. Construction deliveries for medical facilities have decreased over the last eight to twelve quarters due to rising material and labor costs. Office-to-medical conversions are generally not sufficient to meet the needs of large office projects (e.g., 400,000-500,000+ SF buildings).

articles.title: Partnership Breaks Ground on 78-Unit Multifamily Project in Long Branch, New Jersey
articles.narrative: A partnership between Downeaster Development and TANTUM Real Estate has commenced construction on The Elbie, a 78-unit multifamily project in Long Branch, New Jersey. The four-story building will also feature 4,000 square feet of ground-floor retail space and a variety of amenities. Initial occupancy is anticipated in late spring 2027.

data_points.notable_features: Includes one- and two-bedroom units, a fitness center, clubroom, game room, recording and podcast studio, and outdoor grilling and dining stations.

articles.title: JLL Arranges $19.5M Loan for Refinancing of Industrial Building in Westchester County
articles.narrative: JLL has secured a $19.5 million refinancing loan from Webster Bank for a 118,991-square-foot industrial building in Croton-on-Hudson, NY. The property, fully leased to a single tenant, was purpose-built in 1995 for tire distribution and is owned by a partnership between Monday Properties and KPR Centers.

data_points.address: 1 Half Moon Bay Drive, Croton-on-Hudson, NY
data_points.notable_features: Purpose-built for tire distribution with 29-foot clear height, 10 dock doors, and two drive-in doors.

articles.title: Marcus & Millichap Brokers $7.1M Sale of Two Long Island Office Buildings
articles.narrative: Marcus & Millichap has brokered the $7.1 million sale of two office buildings totaling 28,714 square feet in Roslyn, NY. The properties, located at 200-220 S. Service Road, were sold by an undisclosed seller to two separate undisclosed buyers. Marcus & Millichap represented the seller and one of the buyers, while Global Realty Services USA procured the buyer for the second building.

articles.title: Hydrogen Fitness Signs 17,000 SF Retail Lease in Midtown Manhattan
articles.narrative: Hydrogen Fitness has signed a 15-year retail lease for 17,000 square feet in Midtown Manhattan's Murray Hill submarket. The space, located at 145 E. 32nd St., will span the ground, lower, and mezzanine levels of the building. The new fitness center is expected to open in January.

data_points.address: 145 E. 32nd St, New York City, NY
data_points.project_notes: 15-year lease term.

articles.title: Partnership Breaks Ground on 809,141 SF Industrial Park in Durham
articles.narrative: A joint venture between Hines and an Ares Real Estate fund has broken ground on Parkside Commerce Center, an 809,141-square-foot industrial park in Durham, NC. The project will be developed in two phases, with the first phase expected to deliver in the fourth quarter of 2027. Foundry Commercial has been retained to manage leasing and marketing for the new industrial development.

data_points.notable_features: Adjacent to Research Triangle Park. Phase I buildings will feature 36-foot clear heights and 63 trailer stalls. Phase II buildings will have 32-foot clear heights.

articles.title: Sullivan Development to develop 200-unit affordable housing development in Indianapolis
articles.narrative: Sullivan Development has secured financing for Junction at Fall Creek, a 200-unit affordable housing development in Indianapolis. The project, with an estimated total development cost of $50 million, will transform a long-vacant 6-acre former rail yard site into high-quality apartment homes for residents earning 60% or less of the Area Median Income. Construction commenced in May 2026, with completion expected in late 2027, aiming to address the critical need for workforce housing and contribute to neighborhood revitalization.

data_points.notable_features: Located along Fall Creek, where the Monon Trail, Nickel Plate Trail, and Fall Creek Trail system converge, offering riverfront walking paths, community spaces, a playground, dog park, and pickleball court. Convenient access to the Purple Line Bus Rapid Transit corridor.
data_points.project_notes: This project is the first phase of the broader Monon 35 master-planned development. It will provide financial literacy, homeownership planning, workforce development, and food access programming for residents.

articles.title: Not all industrial markets are created equal: The country’s top 25 industrial hubs are gaining strength
articles.narrative: A new report from Colliers indicates that the U.S. industrial sector is transitioning into a more balanced phase, characterized by improving fundamentals and moderating supply. The post-pandemic construction boom has subsided, with new deliveries falling 24% year-over-year and construction activity down 60% from its 2022 peak. This shift is leading to stronger demand and stabilizing vacancy rates, particularly in the top 25 industrial markets which account for 76% of the nation's inventory. While Sun Belt markets like Dallas-Fort Worth and Houston continue to lead in inventory growth and net absorption, several Midwest markets, including Indianapolis, are demonstrating robust fundamentals with demand outpacing supply.

articles.title: SVN|Chicago Commercial brokers sale of 18,000-square-foot industrial property in Chicago
articles.narrative: SVN|Chicago Commercial recently facilitated the sale of an 18,000-square-foot industrial property located at 3450 N. Kostner Ave. in Chicago for $1.433 million. The buyer, a Canadian millwork company, acquired the facility to support its expansion into the Chicago market. The property's robust infrastructure and strategic location were key factors in its appeal as an owner-user opportunity.

data_points.notable_features: Two-story building with high ceilings, heavy power, functional loading, and convenient access to major expressways and public transportation.

articles.title: Cushman & Wakefield brokers $12.25 million sale of mixed-use property in Carmel
articles.narrative: Cushman & Wakefield brokered the $12.25 million sale of Monon & Main, a four-story mixed-use office and retail property in Carmel, Indiana. The transaction, priced at $354 per square foot, established a new record for office sales in the state. The property was fully leased at the time of sale, reflecting strong demand for Class-A+ assets in the Carmel Arts & Design District.

data_points.notable_features: Class-A+ boutique property located in the Carmel Arts & Design District, comprised of 79% office and 21% retail space.

articles.market_intelligence: The transaction set a new high-water mark for office sales in the state of Indiana at $354 per square foot.

articles.title: Massachusetts relaunches effort to redevelop Boston Government Center
articles.narrative: The state of Massachusetts has relaunched its efforts to redevelop the 6.5-acre Boston Government Center complex, comprising the Erich Lindemann Mental Health Center and the Charles F. Hurley Building. The administration of Governor Maura Healey has issued an offering memorandum for the site, which is intended to create new housing and preserve mental health services. This marks a renewed push after a previous $1 billion mixed-use redevelopment plan for the Hurley Building, awarded in 2022, fell through due to a downturn in the life sciences market.

data_points.notable_features: The complex includes the Erich Lindemann Mental Health Center and the Charles F. Hurley Building, both built in the 1960s and in need of significant capital improvements.
data_points.project_notes: The 347,000-square-foot Hurley Building is currently empty, while the Lindemann building still houses state agencies and 115 beds for mental health services, which must be maintained on-site or nearby. Developers are invited to submit proposals with affordability requirements and a focus on historic preservation and adaptive reuse. The site is part of the State Land For Homes inventory, aiming to create housing across income levels.

articles.title: LISTEN: Inside David and Michael Shabsels’ summer camp empire
articles.narrative: This article discusses the financial troubles of Simad Holdings, an operator of approximately 30 summer camps, which recently filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy. The company defaulted on Israeli bonds and transferred $34 million to its owners' private companies, leading to significant liabilities. The situation highlights the risks associated with aggressive financing strategies and has drawn attention from Israeli securities investigators.

articles.title: How Mamdani cut $78B from housing plan, yet added units
articles.narrative: Mayor Zohran Mamdani's updated housing plan aims to create and preserve 400,000 affordable housing units over 10 years with a five-year spending commitment of $22 billion, a significant reduction from his initial $100 billion campaign promise. The revised strategy marks a pivot towards leveraging the private sector for financing and development, relying on market-rate units to subsidize affordable housing rather than extensive public borrowing and union labor. The plan emphasizes upzoning, office conversions, and redeveloping city-owned and publicly controlled sites, while also continuing the use of for-profit firms for public housing renovation and management. The mayor's approach acknowledges that housing scarcity drives gentrification and displacement, aligning with a supply-side strategy to address the city's affordability crisis.

articles.title: NYC’s top deals: Stalled Brookland Capital site trades for $8M
articles.narrative: KG Atlantic Realty Holding Inc. has acquired a stalled residential development site at 929 Atlantic Avenue in Clinton Hill for $7.7 million. The property, which was previously owned by Brookland Capital and later taken over by Amos Financial through foreclosure, is now under the ownership of KG Atlantic Realty Holding Inc., led by president Fei Guo.

data_points.address: 929 Atlantic Avenue, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Stalled residential development site.
data_points.project_notes: Property was previously owned by Brookland Capital and taken over by Amos Financial in 2022 amid a foreclosure case.

articles.title: PolicyPro: Council bill would shield “harassed” properties from 311 complaints, progressives push commercial rent control
articles.narrative: A new City Council bill in New York City aims to protect "harassed" properties from repeated, unsubstantiated 311 complaints by requiring agencies to stop inspections after three anonymous complaints within six months that do not result in verified violations. This measure seeks to prevent the misuse of the 311 system, which can negatively impact property owners by raising red flags for lenders, insurers, and potential buyers, even when no violations are found. Separately, progressive City Council members are advocating for the state Legislature to approve commercial rent stabilization for New York City, proposing a system with a Commercial Rent Guidelines Board and minimum 10-year lease renewals to support small businesses facing rising rents.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: Small Property Owners of New York

articles.title: Haruvi family company files for bankruptcy, affecting nearly 500 apartments
articles.narrative: Simry Realty Corp, a company linked to the Haruvi family, has filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy, impacting nearly 500 apartments across seven Manhattan properties. The bankruptcy filing aims to halt a protracted legal battle with family member Michelle Haruvi, who claims she was unfairly excluded from a 2022 company restructuring. This litigation has reportedly hindered the company's ability to refinance its mortgages, which matured in June 2024.

data_points.address: 309 to 315 West 54th Street, New York City, NY; 38 and 54 West 75th Street, New York City, NY; 244 West 74th Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Seven Manhattan properties are affected by the bankruptcy filing.
data_points.project_notes: The company's mortgages matured in June 2024. The bankruptcy filing is intended to stay ongoing litigation related to a 2022 restructuring deal.

articles.title: Hawkins Way expands student housing bet with UWS buy
articles.narrative: Hawkins Way Capital has acquired the Stratford Residence Hall, a student housing property on Manhattan's Upper West Side, from AMDA College of the Performing Arts for $80 million. The performing arts college will lease back the 382-unit property for the next 30 years. This transaction marks another significant student housing investment for Hawkins Way Capital in the New York City market, following several other notable acquisitions and dispositions in the sector.

data_points.address: 117 West 70th Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: The property previously served as a migrant shelter.
data_points.project_notes: AMDA College of the Performing Arts will lease back the property for 30 years.

companies_people.label: SELLER
companies_people.firm_name: AMDA College of the Performing Arts

companies_people.label: TENANT
companies_people.firm_name: AMDA College of the Performing Arts

articles.title: Rivani sells Wynwood Jungle for $26M to fund next Miami Beach buy
articles.narrative: Robert Rivani's firm sold the Wynwood Jungle, a nearly 39,000-square-foot retail plaza in Miami, for $26 million to a Dallas-based investment group. The sale price is nearly double its acquisition cost five years prior, despite the property being 50 percent vacant and facing ongoing lease disputes with former tenants. The proceeds from this sale will be used to finance Rivani's upcoming acquisition of an iconic office building in Miami Beach, signaling a strategic shift away from retail and hospitality assets.

data_points.notable_features: Includes a 10,000-square-foot rooftop space with significant upside potential for a new tenant.
data_points.project_notes: Previously known as Wynwood Arcade. Acquired in 2021 for $13 million. Seller retained a small portion of the property, which is under contract for $1 million.

articles.title: Florida Keys military housing lawsuit against Balfour Beatty goes to federal court
articles.narrative: A mass action lawsuit against Balfour Beatty and its subsidiaries, alleging unsafe housing conditions for military families at Naval Air Station Key West, has advanced to federal court. The complaint claims the firm profited while residents lived in water-damaged, moldy, and pest-infested homes, seeking damages for alleged negligence. This legal action follows a previous lawsuit in Monroe County and a 2021 case where a Balfour Beatty subsidiary pleaded guilty to defrauding the U.S. military regarding housing records.

data_points.project_notes: The lawsuit alleges hazardous living conditions including collapsed water-damaged ceilings, insect infestations, plumbing and electrical issues, lead paint, mold, and asbestos.

articles.title: South Florida real estate bets on World Cup for slow season boost
articles.narrative: South Florida's real estate and hospitality sectors are anticipating a significant boost from the FIFA World Cup, which is expected to draw 700,000 visitors and generate $1.3 billion in spending. With seven matches scheduled at Hard Rock Stadium, developers, hoteliers, and retailers across Miami-Dade and Broward counties are implementing various fan activations and marketing campaigns to capitalize on the event. This influx of visitors is projected to extend the typically slow summer season, with residential developers noting an uptick in foreign buyer interest and hoteliers reporting increased bookings and average daily rates compared to previous years.

articles.title: Miami Wilds water park project crashes after judge nixes dev site lease due to “mutual mistake”
articles.narrative: A Miami-Dade Circuit Court judge has voided the lease agreement for the controversial Miami Wilds water park project near Zoo Miami, effectively halting the mixed-use development. The judge ruled that the lease was based on a "mutual mistake" between Miami-Dade County and Miami Wilds LLC, as a federal court had previously invalidated a key amendment that lifted development restrictions on the site. The county is ordered to refund nearly $257,000 in rent to the developers, who are now evaluating their next steps after over a decade of planning and litigation.

data_points.original_plan: 100+ acre project with a theme park in partnership with 20th Century Studios
data_points.notable_features: Planned water park, hotel, and commercial/dining space adjacent to Zoo Miami; site is part of the Richmond Pine Rockland tract, a biodiverse pine rockland habitat.
data_points.project_notes: The development site was leased from Miami-Dade County, which had received the land from the federal government with restrictions against commercial development. A 2022 amendment to a 2011 agreement between the county and the National Park Service, intended to lift these restrictions, was later voided by a federal court in December 2023, leading to the current lease rescission.

articles.title: Highgate Hotels sells downtown Chicago La Quinta for $31M
articles.narrative: Highgate Hotels, in a joint venture with Cerberus Capital Management, sold the 239-room La Quinta hotel in downtown Chicago for $30.5 million to JAMP Hotels. This sale is part of Highgate's strategy to offload properties from a $1.5 billion portfolio acquired in 2021, particularly as a $625 million loan against 29 of these hotels faced imminent monetary default. JAMP Hotels plans to invest in revamping the property's amenities, expressing strong confidence in the future of Chicago's downtown hospitality sector, which has shown promising tourism recovery post-pandemic.

data_points.notable_features: Includes two groundfloor restaurant spaces with long-term leases.
data_points.project_notes: Part of a portfolio of La Quinta-branded hotels acquired by Highgate and Cerberus Capital Management in 2021 for $1.5 billion. A $625 million loan against 29 properties in the portfolio was moved to special servicing in February 2025 due to imminent monetary default.

articles.market_intelligence: Chicago's hospitality sector saw 3.6 million room nights filled downtown in June, July, and August of 2025, a 4% increase over 2024 and higher than the pre-pandemic record set in 2019. The city's tourism board is implementing a 1.5% surcharge to double the budget of tourism agency Choose Chicago, a measure self-imposed by downtown hotel owners.

articles.title: Oak Brook medical office space purchased by its tenant from Harrison Street and MedProperties Group
articles.narrative: A Northwestern Medicine affiliate has acquired a seven-story medical office building in Oak Brook, Illinois, for $58 million, transitioning from tenant to owner. The property, located at 1001 Commerce Drive, was sold by its developers, Harrison Street and MedProperties Group. This transaction highlights the strong demand for medical office space in the Chicagoland area, a sector that continues to see consistent activity and investment despite broader office market challenges.

data_points.project_notes: The developers previously acquired the site for $4.2 million in 2021 and secured a $37 million construction loan in the same year.

articles.market_intelligence: Medical office and lab property deals in Chicagoland totaled over $600 million in May. The demand for outpatient space remains consistent, contrasting with general office properties that have struggled with vacancy due to hybrid work trends. The medical office market is stable due to an aging population and low supply, though investors are cautious with federally funded offices.

articles.title: Highland Park lakefront mansion fetches highest price since Jordan estate
articles.narrative: A lakefront residence in Highland Park, IL, was sold by Jerry and Naomi Senser for $6.5 million, marking the highest sale price in the suburb since Michael Jordan's estate traded. The 9,750-square-foot home, situated on 1.18 acres, features a private beach, boathouse, and deck, with significant restoration work completed on the shoreline. The property was on the market for approximately one year, closing at 74% of its initial asking price.

data_points.address: 55 South Deere Park Drive, Highland Park, IL
data_points.notable_features: Features a private beach, boathouse, and deck, with a retaining wall to protect the shoreline from erosion.
data_points.project_notes: The property was previously acquired by the sellers in 2006 for $6.5 million. Beach and bluff restoration work was completed in 2009 and 2015.

articles.title: Huntington Bank goes against the grain in the Loop
articles.narrative: Huntington Bank has significantly expanded its office footprint in Chicago's Loop, signing a 10-year lease for 60,900 square feet at 222 North LaSalle Street. This move nearly doubles the bank's previous space in the building, bucking the trend of office space reduction in the market. The expansion is attributed to recent renovations by the building owners, which have enhanced the property's appeal to tenants.

data_points.notable_features: Building features an improved lobby and new refreshment offerings.
data_points.project_notes: Huntington Bank's new lease is for a decade.

articles.market_intelligence: The downtown office market in Chicago has an average occupancy rate 15% lower than 222 North LaSalle Street's 87% occupancy. Redevelopment efforts are transforming vacant office space into hotels, multifamily units, or retail space across downtown Chicago.

articles.title: Fairmont Austin’s $430M CMBS loan flagged for special servicing amid foreclosure flurry
articles.narrative: The $430 million CMBS loan for the Fairmont Austin, owned by Manchester Financial Group, has been transferred to special servicing due to a significant decline in cash flow and occupancy. This luxury hotel, which opened in 2018 and was refinanced in 2024, is facing financial distress, exacerbated by the ongoing closure of the adjacent Austin Convention Center for renovations. The situation at Fairmont Austin is part of a broader trend of distress among Downtown Austin hotels, with two other prominent properties recently facing foreclosure or handing keys back to lenders.

data_points.notable_features: Connected to the Austin Convention Center by skybridge.
data_points.project_notes: Loan was used to refinance the property in 2024. The property was listed for sale in 2023 for over $600 million.

articles.market_intelligence: Downtown Austin's luxury hotel market is experiencing distress, with the Fairmont Austin being the third hotel in two months to face financial challenges. The Line Austin, a 428-key hotel at 111 East Cesar Chavez Street, was handed back to its lender after an alleged default on a $172 million loan provided by JP Morgan in 2023. The Hyatt Centric also lost control during a foreclosure auction after defaulting on a $50.5 million loan. The Austin Convention Center, located at 500 East Cesar Chavez Street, closed for a $1.6 million overhaul in 2025, doubling its meeting space to 700,000 square feet, and is expected to reopen in 2029.

articles.title: King Ranch scion sells historic Houston home
articles.narrative: John Steen III and Mary Steen sold their historic 1931 home at 1419 Kirby Drive in Houston to attorney Ryan Pigg. The five-bedroom, seven-bathroom residence spans 6,300 square feet on a half-acre lot in the River Oaks neighborhood. The property, which was once considered for city landmark status, went under contract in just 14 days after being listed for $6.5 million, making it one of Houston's top residential sales in May.

data_points.notable_features: Designed by Katharine Mott, the city's first woman developer; received a preservation award in 2022 for its restoration.
data_points.project_notes: The home was at one point a candidate for city landmark status.

articles.market_intelligence: The typical Houston luxury home went under contract after a median of 40 days on market in the spring. River Oaks accounted for five of the top 10 most expensive residential listings sold in Harris County last year.

articles.title: Stars CEO says tear down American Airlines Center once teams leave
articles.narrative: The CEO of the Dallas Stars has publicly advocated for the demolition of the American Airlines Center once both the Stars and Mavericks vacate the arena in 2031. This recommendation comes as both teams plan to construct new, separate arenas: the Mavericks in Valley View, Dallas, and the Stars in Plano. The Stars' proposed $3 billion arena and entertainment district in Plano, developed by Centennial Real Estate and Cawley Partners, recently received city council approval for preliminary studies, with Plano offering significant financial incentives. Dallas city officials, however, are exploring options to retain the Stars within the city.

data_points.project_notes: Leases for both the Dallas Stars and Dallas Mavericks expire in 2031. The Stars considered renovating the AAC but found the cost comparable to new construction.

articles.title: Former Spurs star LaMarcus Aldridge buys another Westlake home
articles.narrative: Former NBA player LaMarcus Aldridge, through his LLC, has acquired a 10,466-square-foot mansion in Westlake, Texas, from Larry and Maria Smith. The property, initially listed for $10.9 million, was under contract at $10 million. This acquisition further expands Aldridge's real estate portfolio, which is primarily concentrated in the Dallas-Fort Worth area.

data_points.notable_features: Located in the Quail Hollow subdivision near the Vaquero country club, featuring a six-car garage, outdoor kitchen, and basketball court.

articles.title: Monty Bennett’s Ashford secures $480M payout in Braemar divorce, spurring self-dealing claims
articles.narrative: Braemar Hotels and Resorts is transitioning to a self-managed REIT after severing ties with Ashford Inc., a move that will result in a $480 million payout to Ashford. This separation follows allegations of self-dealing and conflicts of interest from Braemar's largest shareholder, Al Shams Investments, who claims the asset sales were engineered to trigger the termination fee benefiting Ashford and its chairman, Monty Bennett. Braemar has already sold several hotel properties, including the Park Hyatt Beaver Creek Resort & Spa, and plans to sell additional assets to finalize the separation.

data_points.property_name: Park Hyatt Beaver Creek Resort & Spa
data_points.project_notes: Braemar Hotels and Resorts is liquidating 14 properties as part of its separation from Ashford Inc. and plans to sell additional assets. The Park Hyatt Beaver Creek Resort & Spa was sold in April 2026, and other properties in Florida, New Mexico, and Virginia were also offloaded. Two other hotel sales are pending.

articles.title: San Francisco’s mansion shortage claims two more trophy homes
articles.narrative: Two trophy homes in San Francisco's Presidio Heights neighborhood recently traded for a combined $32 million, highlighting the city's tightening luxury housing market. The transactions included the $21 million sale of a five-bedroom mansion by Richard and Monene Bradley to 3501 Jackson LLC, and the $11 million purchase of another five-bedroom mansion by tech executive Woodrow Levin and his wife Gretchen from David Brailer and Matthew Rhoa. These sales contribute to a trend of increasing luxury home transactions in the area, fueled by wealth generated from the artificial intelligence industry.

articles.title: Sunnyvale offices once home to Google poised for housing conversion
articles.narrative: Dallas-based real estate firm Beam Reach has proposed demolishing a former Google office building at 215 Bordeaux Drive in Sunnyvale, CA, to construct a 265-unit residential building. The Sunnyvale planning commissioners have unanimously approved the proposal. This project is part of a broader trend in the Bay Area to convert underutilized office space into housing, driven by high housing demand and the city's mandate to plan for new housing units.

data_points.notable_features: Eight-story multifamily building replacing a one-story research and development building.
data_points.project_notes: The site was previously a Google office, listed for sublease three years prior as Google trimmed its office footprint.

articles.market_intelligence: Sunnyvale's office vacancy rate is 10.9%, compared to 15.4% across Silicon Valley. San Mateo County is seeing increased demolition of underutilized offices for housing. Sunnyvale is required to plan for 11,966 new housing units by 2031, with Moffett Park identified as a key area for conversion into mixed-use neighborhoods.

articles.title: Belmont developer plans 202 apartments in neighboring San Mateo
articles.narrative: The McLellan Company is moving forward with plans to develop a six-story, 202-unit multifamily building at 4095 Pacific Boulevard in San Mateo, CA. The project will include 25 deed-restricted affordable units and will benefit from state density bonus laws and streamlined review under Senate Bill 330. The development will replace an existing gas station and shopping center, contributing to San Mateo's state-mandated housing goals.

data_points.notable_features: Includes 25 deed-restricted affordable units and benefits from streamlined review under Senate Bill 330 and state density bonus law.
data_points.project_notes: Existing gas station and one-story shopping center will be demolished.

articles.market_intelligence: The City of San Mateo must plan for 7,015 new housing units by 2031. Other recent multifamily developments in San Mateo include Prometheus Real Estate Group's 128-unit Gateway project at 668 East Third Avenue, Align Real Estate's 396-unit project at 1655 South El Camino Real, and Tourbineau Real Estate Partners' 144-unit office-to-residential conversion at 2121 South El Camino Real.

articles.title: Investor Offloads Dunwoody Office Park for $27M
articles.narrative: Grubb Properties has sold the 284,000-square-foot Park at Perimeter Center East in Dunwoody, GA, to Network Realty Partners for $27.15 million. Grubb Properties had acquired the 19.4-acre site in 2015 for $32.65 million and had previously attempted to redevelop the property with a large mixed-use project, but those plans did not receive local approval. The sale marks Grubb Properties' exit from this particular asset, as the company is also relocating its Atlanta office from the Dunwoody property to another office it owns in Smyrna.

data_points.project_notes: Seller previously acquired the 19.4-acre site for $32.65 million in 2015 and had proposed a redevelopment plan in 2018 for 900 for-sale condos and 500,000 square feet of office space, which was not approved.

articles.title: RADCO Obtains $53M Refi on Atlanta Hotel
articles.narrative: The RADCO Companies secured a $53 million refinancing loan for The American Hotel Atlanta Downtown, a 315-key hospitality asset in downtown Atlanta. The financing, arranged by Newmark, was provided by Structured Hotel Capital. The hotel recently underwent an $11.3 million renovation to modernize its facilities.

data_points.address: 160 Ted Turner Drive NW, Atlanta, GA
data_points.notable_features: Affiliated with Hilton's Tapestry Collection, features 13,300 square feet of meeting space, an outdoor pool, and fitness center. Located in the Fairlie District, close to major attractions like the Georgia World Congress Center and Mercedes-Benz Stadium.
data_points.project_notes: The hotel completed an $11.3 million renovation prior to this refinancing.

articles.title: CBRE Arranges Sale of Newly Renovated 113K-SF Indiana Industrial Property
articles.narrative: Vault Equity Partners has acquired a 112,862-square-foot industrial property in Elkhart, Indiana, from Industrial Commercial Properties (ICP). The asset, a former JCPenney retail location, was fully renovated and converted into a distribution facility, demonstrating continued investor demand for quality single-tenant industrial assets in the Midwest. The property was fully leased at the time of sale.

data_points.notable_features: Former JCPenney retail location fully renovated and converted into a distribution facility.
data_points.project_notes: ICP redeveloped the former Concord Mall into Concord Business Park, converting the JCPenney into a freestanding industrial facility.

articles.title: Michael Puline: Principal’s Mindset is Essential to Modern Brokerage
articles.narrative: Michael Puline, a veteran in retail real estate, has joined Marcus & Millichap and Institutional Property Advisors (IPA) to spearhead the strategic growth of their retail divisions and lead investment sales professionals. With a 25-year background in closing over 2,500 leasing and sale transactions totaling $8.5 billion, Puline aims to leverage his principal's mindset, gained from two decades on the ownership side, to enhance client service and execution. He believes the future of brokerage requires a deep understanding of the capital stack, operational challenges, and long-term business strategy, moving beyond mere transaction execution. Puline was drawn to the firm's extensive network, proprietary data, and integrated research, which he sees as crucial advantages in the fast-paced retail market. He notes that retail has transitioned from a doubted asset class to one with strong fundamentals, driven by consistent demand for physical footprints, including last-mile retail and service-oriented businesses. While grocery-anchored and net-lease properties remain strong, small shop demand is significantly driving rental income growth, leading to increased interest in unanchored strips. Retailers are also showing greater flexibility in formats and market strategies, adapting to limited supply and becoming more receptive to higher rents due to improved sales and margins.

articles.title: Brexit Slowly But Surely Remade London Commercial Real Estate. Now The Future Is Even Less Certain
articles.narrative: A decade after the Brexit vote, London's commercial real estate market has been fundamentally reshaped, with investment volumes and leasing activity failing to return to pre-2016 levels. The political instability unleashed by Brexit, coupled with subsequent geopolitical and macroeconomic shocks, has made buyers wary and shifted the investor landscape, with institutional buyers decreasing significantly. While London retains its appeal and has seen strong rent growth for prime offices and an increase in office jobs, the market's future remains deeply uncertain amidst higher interest rates and slower growth compared to continental European counterparts.

articles.title: 'We're In A Different Cycle': Fund Managers Try To Convince Investors OZ 2.0 Will Be Better
articles.narrative: Opportunity Zone fund managers are actively working to reassure investors that the upcoming OZ 2.0 program, with its revised structure and more favorable market timing, will yield stronger returns compared to the initial iteration. The first version of the program faced significant headwinds from the pandemic and rising interest rates, which impacted investor profitability. Key changes in OZ 2.0 include its permanence in the tax code, a new map of qualified census tracts, and enhanced tax incentives for investments in rural areas. Fund managers like GTIS Partners are emphasizing that the underperformance of some early OZ funds was primarily due to market cycle timing rather than inherent flaws in the program itself, and they are educating investors on the new rules and the current real estate cycle.

articles.title: What CRE Executives Want In An Increasingly Fraught Job Market
articles.narrative: Commercial real estate executives are seeking new college graduates with a broader and more diverse skill set than in previous decades, as the job market for recent grads faces its highest unemployment rate in a decade. Developers emphasized the need for new hires to possess multidisciplinary expertise, including financial engineering, design, sustainability, and proficiency in integrating artificial intelligence into workflows. Some firms are also prioritizing candidates with prior government experience, particularly in affordable housing, due to the increasing complexity and nuanced nature of such projects. The consensus among industry leaders is that while specialized knowledge remains important, the ability to think critically, adapt to new technologies, and demonstrate a wide range of transferable skills is crucial for new entrants to stand out.

articles.title: Shorenstein Investment Advisers Buys Premium Office Campus In Major Dallas Market
articles.narrative: Shorenstein Investment Advisers has acquired The Tennyson, a 273,000-square-foot Class-A office campus in Plano's Legacy business park, from Spear Street Capital. The fully leased, two-building campus, originally developed in 2012 as a corporate headquarters, recently underwent significant capital improvements. Shorenstein plans further amenity enhancements, aligning with its strategy to invest in submarkets poised for rent growth and strong demographic tailwinds. The acquisition reflects continued investor appetite for high-quality office product in growing Sun Belt markets, particularly assets with recent reinvestment and strong market positioning.

data_points.notable_features: Class-A office campus with upgraded amenities including conference facilities, tenant lounge, cafe, speakeasy, and outdoor space.
data_points.project_notes: The campus underwent significant capital improvements in 2024. Shorenstein plans further amenity improvements, including more outdoor offerings.

articles.market_intelligence: Plano's office market is experiencing growth due to corporate relocations, including AT&T moving its global headquarters to Plano in 2028 and Samsung Electronics America relocating its U.S. headquarters to Plano by the end of 2026. AT&T's new headquarters will be a $1.35 billion project, more than double the size of its current 1 million SF building. The Dallas Stars also announced plans for a mixed-use district anchored by a new arena in Plano.

articles.title: DataBank Closes On $1.45B In Financing For 2 Deals: The DFW Deal Sheet
articles.narrative: DataBank, a Dallas-based data center provider, has secured over $1.4 billion in new financing to bolster its capital structure and support ongoing expansion. The financing includes an $800 million revolving credit facility maturing in 2031 and a $650 million upsize to its existing construction financing for its 300-acre data center campus in Red Oak, Texas. This strategic financial move, which also incorporates DataBank's first private placement transaction, positions the company to capitalize on accelerating demand for data center capacity from hyperscale, cloud, and enterprise customers.

data_points.notable_features: The upsize supports the construction of a fourth building, adding 60 megawatts of incremental capacity.
data_points.project_notes: The financing includes an $800M revolving credit facility and a $650M upsize to an existing $2B construction financing. The upsize consists of $400M in bank financing and $250M of notes in DataBank’s first private placement transaction.
